exemplar.title: The investigation of the influence of temperature on the spring constant of a spring

detailedExemplarData.title: The investigation of the influence of temperature on the spring constant of a spring

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graph are wellâlabelled with units and uncertainties, showing clear overall presentation.
Uncertainty propagation for extension and spring constant is correctly applied.
Spring constant values and units are consistent, and the negative linear trend is clearly supported by Pearsonâs r.
weaknesses: Several data cells (initial lengths) are left blank, which may confuse readers.
Variable notation (L_E vs L_g) is inconsistent across calculations.
Calculated gradient uncertainty does not align with plotted error margins and needs reevaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands of the dataâanalysis criterion are in the 3â4 performance band: communication is generally clear though not fully precise, uncertainties are considered with some omissions, and processing is largely accurate. A bandâtop mark of 4 reflects solid analytical work with minor imperfections.

strengths: Introduction links study to realâworld observations and states a clear, focused aim.
Research question is placed in a specific context with relevant theory (Hookeâs law, thermal expansion).
Materials list, method steps and variable tables are detailed enough for reproducibility with minor ambiguities.
weaknesses: Source and control of sub-zero and elevated temperatures are not clearly described.
Time allowed for thermal equilibration is omitted, introducing minor reproducibility issues.
Hypothesis phrasing is vague (âcorrelationâ rather than direction) and could be more precise.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands (method description and methodological considerations) fall within the 3â4 band, while the researchâquestion context strand meets the 5â6 band. Averaging these performances yields a holistic score of 4, reflecting generally strong design with some omissions preventing a topâband mark.

strengths: Conclusion states the observed relationship and attempts to relate it to analysis.
Literature comparison to NiâTi spring behavior is relevant and shows awareness of scientific context.
weaknesses: Terminology âdirect relationshipâ contradicts the observed negative slope.
Conclusion lacks quantitative support from regression or uncertainty analysis.
Literature comparison is brief and not fully integrated with numerical findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: One strand of the conclusion criterion sits in the 1â2 band (weak or unsupported conclusion) and the other in the 3â4 band (some relevant scientific comparison). A holistic score of 3 balances the partial support and context against inconsistencies and lack of quantitative justification.

strengths: Specific methodological limitations (uneven heating, narrow range, small sample) are identified with impact statements.
Realistic improvements (use of water bath, broader temperature range, larger sample) are proposed for each limitation.
weaknesses: Does not evaluate the relative impact or priority of the identified weaknesses.
Suggested improvements lack quantitative justification (e.g., how much uncertainty would decrease).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both evaluation strands are in the 3â4 band: weaknesses and improvements are described but not fully explained in terms of relative impact or detailed feasibility. A midâband score of 3 reflects an adequate but not inâdepth evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The introduction links the study to realâworld observations (slinky behaviour), states a clear aim and gives relevant theory (Hookeâs law, thermal expansion).  The reader is left in no doubt about why temperature is a meaningful factor for k and what precise temperature range will be explored.  This constitutes a specific and appropriate context rather than a merely broad outline.  Minor stylistic issues do not detract from contextual clarity, so the strand best fits the 5â6 band; a mid-band score of 5 is appropriate.

Uncertainties are propagated for one worked example, included for every mean extension and for each k value, and plotted as error bars.  Gradient uncertainty is attempted, but the calculation is acknowledged as inconsistent.  Random and systematic sources are not analysed in depth.  Hence there is evidence with âsome significant omissionsâ, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects the partial but incomplete treatment.

The student identifies dependent, independent and controlled variables and notes uncontrolled ones.  Risks and equipment tolerances are presented.  However, the justification of why 0.041 kg was chosen, why five trials are statistically sufficient, or how the temperature range ensures ârelevant and sufficientâ data is only touched on.  Thus methodological considerations are described but not fully explained, matching the descriptors for 3â4; a mid-band mark of 3 reflects these partial explanations.

Three concrete weaknesses are identified (uneven heating, limited range, small sample).  Each is described along with a brief impact statement, but the relative magnitude or priority of these limitations is not analysed.  Hence the work meets the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ, placing it in the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects the adequate but not in-depth discussion.

The materials list, step-by-step method, variable tables and sketches give enough information for another student to reproduce the work, but a few ambiguities remain: the source of the sub-zero and +6 Â°C âenvironmental conditionsâ is not described, the number of washers actually hung is unclear, and the time allowed for thermal equilibration is vague.  Because these omissions are minor rather than major, the description allows reproduction "with few ambiguities", placing it in the 3â4 band; a high-band score of 4 is warranted.

Data processing (averages, propagation, calculation of k, linear regression, Pearson coefficient) is appropriate and largely accurate.  Only one worked calculation is shown, yet the final values and units are consistent.  Minor slips (labelling the relationship as linear but then calling it âminuteâ) do not undermine overall correctness.  Therefore the processing satisfies the âappropriate and accurateâ description more than it fails; a band-top mark of 4 is awarded.

For each weakness, the student suggests a realistic improvement (water bath, broader temperature range, larger sample) and gives a short rationale.  The proposals are relevant but not developed into detailed procedures or justified for feasibility, so they are âdescribedâ rather than fully âexplainedâ.  Thus the 3â4 band applies; a mid-band mark of 3 is awarded.

Raw-data and processed-data tables are included with appropriate headings, units and uncertainties, and a clearly labelled graph is provided.  Some cells (initial lengths at several temperatures) are blank, and the graphâs legend partly obscures detail, so the presentation is clear but not perfectly precise.  This fits the descriptor âeither clear or preciseâ, so the 3â4 band is appropriate; a score of 4 rewards generally strong but slightly imperfect communication.

A literature reference to a Ni-Ti study is provided and qualitative similarities/differences are discussed.  The comparison is relevant but brief and not fully integrated with the studentâs numerical findings, so it goes beyond superficial mention but stops short of a thorough justification.  This aligns with the 3â4 descriptor; a mid-band mark of 3 is suitable.

The student states that âthere is a direct relationship â¦ increasing temperature would result in a smaller spring constant.â  The wording âdirectâ contradicts the negative gradient, and no quantitative comparison (e.g., referencing the regression line) is offered.  Support from the analysis is thus weak and partially inconsistent, fitting the 1â2 band; the clearer elements justify the upper score of 2.

annotations.comment: The title is clear but could be more specific by mentioning the temperature range studied to orient the reader immediately.
The personal anecdote and link to prior experiments make the introduction engaging and illustrate genuine curiosity.
The informal tone (ânothing better to doâ) detracts from academic style. Consider rephrasing for formality.
The research question specifies five temperatures clearly, aiding reproducibility and focus.
Clarify how âenvironmental conditionsâ were controlled to reach sub-zero and elevated temperatures to remove ambiguity.
The inclusion of Hookeâs law with clear definitions of F, x and k provides solid theoretical grounding.
The background could be strengthened by quantifying expected thermal expansion of the spring material.
Figure 1 schematic effectively visualizes the setup, aiding comprehension of forces involved.
Caption could note the defined coordinate axes and labeling conventions to enhance clarity.
The derivation of $$k=F/x$$ and $$F=mg$$ is correct and clearly presented.
When combining equations, explicitly state units at each step to prevent unitâconsistency errors.
The hypothesis is vague (âThere is a correlationâ). Specify expected direction and rationale.
Variable table lists methods and uncertainties well, but the number of washers and their total mass should be restated.
Controlled variables table clearly identifies significance and control methods.
The uncontrolled variables description is comprehensive but could note quantitative humidity or airflow ranges.
Materials list is thorough; include calibration dates for measurement devices to reinforce reliability.
Figure 3 experimental setup diagram is clear, showing arrangement of stand, spring, and measuring devices.
Method description omits time allowed for spring to reach each new temperature; specify equilibration period.
Five trials are acceptable but the choice of sample size lacks justification; discuss statistical sufficiency.
Raw data table has clear headers but blanks in initial length column could cause confusion; complete all entries.
Calculation of extension $$L_E=L_f-L_i$$ is correct, but the variable name changes to $$L_g$$ create inconsistency.
Uncertainty propagation for extension is correctly applied, demonstrating understanding of error addition.
Propagation of average extension uncertainty using (maxâmin)/2 is sound, though explaining choice of method would add rigor.
Spring constant calculation uses correct formula and error propagation, yielding consistent units.
Table 7 presents k vs temperature clearly; discuss why uncertainty at 22 Â°C is lower than at other points.
Graph shows a strong negative linear trend with Pearson r = â0.9795, effectively supporting the analysis.
Calculating gradient uncertainty yields a value inconsistent with plotted error margins. Reevaluate the max/min lines chosen.
Terminology âdirect relationshipâ contradicts observed negative slope. Use âinverseâ or ânegativeâ for accuracy.
Literature comparison to Ni-Ti springs is relevant; integrate quantitative contrast with your measured slopes.
The evaluation identifies key limitations but does not assess which has greatest impact on accuracy.
Suggested improvements are realistic; explain how increasing sample size will quantitatively reduce uncertainty.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
rubricData.name: Physics IA
